nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich reefs in the Western Indian Ocean, from the Gulf of Aden south to Natal, South Africa, including Seychelles, Comoros, and Madagascar. Typically encountered at depths of roughly 1–25 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on algae, small invertebrates, and sponge-based matter. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of Spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and other meaty items. Access to mature live rock for grazing is generally advised.

summary: A striking Western Indian Ocean angelfish with a yellow tail, tan body, and blue highlights. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty; semi-aggressive and best kept singly in a large, mature system.
careNotes: The Chrysurus Angelfish (also widely traded as the Goldtail Angelfish and occasionally the Ear Spot Angelfish) is a large Pomacanthus species from the Western Indian Ocean, ranging from the Gulf of Aden south to Natal, South Africa. FishBase notes it is seldom seen in the aquarium trade, making it an uncommonly available species. LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 220 US gallons (~833 L); the nearest nice-value cautious rounding is 1000 L, appropriate for a fish that reaches 33 cm and requires ample open swimming space with substantial live rock. Temperature recommendations differ between sources — LiveAquaria suggests roughly 72–78°F (22–26°C) while FishBase records a wild preference of 26–28.9°C; an averaged working range of approximately 24–27°C is used here. Salinity is generally kept at 1.020–1.025 SG, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. Like other large Pomacanthus angelfishes, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite. LiveAquaria notes it is "hermaphroditic with no visible sex differentiation." Juveniles reportedly retain a clear tail until approximately 3–4 inches, at which point they develop the characteristic yellow coloration of the adult; juveniles and adults can be visually quite distinct. This species has been known to nip at stony and soft corals and clam mantles, so it is generally not considered fully reef-safe; some keepers report success with small-polyped stony corals and noxious soft corals, but caution is advised. Large Pomacanthus angels of this kind tend to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when fed a poor diet or kept in marginal water quality, so frequent feedings with marine algae, vitamin-soaked foods, and stable water chemistry are generally advised. A notable behavioral trait: when stressed, this species has been documented making an unusual clicking sound. Best kept as the only angelfish in the system. Conspecific aggression toward other angelfish species tends to be high.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered extremely difficult and has not been reliably documented for home aquariums. In the wild this species spawns pelagically as a protogynous hermaphrodite. No home-aquarium breeding protocol is described in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max size (33.0 cm SL, consistent with LiveAquaria's 13 inches), native range (Western Indian Ocean, Gulf of Aden to Natal, South Africa, including Seychelles, Comoros, Madagascar), depth (1–25 m), and temperature (26–28.9°C). Temperature is higher than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); averaged to 24–27°C. Notes species is seldom seen in the aquarium trade, reef-associated, non-migratory, and rated Least Concern by IUCN.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 Forbidden; replaced by FishBase as secondary. FishBase confirmed all high-stakes fields independently.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner in the wild; captive breeding has not been reliably documented for home aquariums. LiveAquaria notes it is difficult to breed in captivity.
